idioms.definition: To permanently destroy a relationship or opportunity in a way that cannot be undone; to make it impossible to go back to a previous situation
examples: She burned her bridges at the firm by publicly criticising the senior partners.
Be careful – if you leave on bad terms, you'll burn your bridges.
He burned his bridges by accepting the counteroffer without telling his current employer.
idioms.usageTip: Usually used as a warning. Burning bridges means you cannot retreat – the path back is gone. Historically, armies would burn bridges behind them to prevent retreat.

idioms.definition: To take an irreversible step; to pass a point of no return in a decision or course of action
examples: By filing the lawsuit, she crossed the Rubicon – there was no settling out of court now.
Announcing the policy publicly meant the government had crossed the Rubicon.
He crossed the Rubicon the moment he sent the resignation letter.
idioms.usageTip: From Julius Caesar's crossing of the Rubicon river in 49 BC, which was a declaration of war. More formal and dramatic than 'the die is cast'. Both idioms convey irreversibility.

idioms.definition: To experience a decisive and final defeat; to encounter the one challenge or opponent that you cannot overcome
examples: The unbeaten chess champion finally met his Waterloo against the young prodigy.
The ambitious politician met her Waterloo in the second debate.
Every empire, eventually, meets its Waterloo.
idioms.usageTip: Named after the Battle of Waterloo in 1815, where Napoleon suffered his final defeat. Suggests a decisive, humbling, and often unexpected downfall.

idioms.definition: A burden or source of guilt that constantly weighs on you and is difficult or impossible to escape
examples: The failed investment became an albatross around the company's neck.
His past criminal record was an albatross around his neck.
The scandal has been an albatross around the minister's neck for years.
idioms.usageTip: From Coleridge's 'The Rime of the Ancient Mariner', in which a sailor who kills an albatross is forced to wear it around his neck as punishment. Used for persistent, inescapable burdens.

idioms.definition: A constant threat or danger that hangs over someone; a feeling of imminent disaster or potential catastrophe
examples: The threat of litigation hung over the company like the sword of Damocles.
He spent years under the sword of Damocles, waiting to hear whether the appeal would succeed.
The possibility of redundancy is the sword of Damocles for many workers.
idioms.usageTip: From the classical story of Damocles, who sat beneath a sword suspended by a single horse hair. Used in formal contexts for persistent existential threat or anxiety.

idioms.definition: A source of many unforeseen and problematic consequences; something that, once opened, causes a cascade of troubles
examples: Legalising one exception opened Pandora's box of further claims.
The investigation opened Pandora's box and exposed corruption at every level.
That question opened Pandora's box – the debate went on for hours.
idioms.usageTip: From Greek mythology – Pandora opened a box releasing all the evils of the world. Similar in use to 'open a can of worms' but more formal, dramatic, and often larger in scale.

fillInExercises.sentence: Don't ___ by leaving without proper notice. You may need a reference from them one day.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Burn your bridges' means to permanently destroy a relationship in a way that cannot be undone.

fillInExercises.sentence: The debt from the failed venture became ___ that he struggled to escape for a decade.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'An albatross around your neck' is a persistent, inescapable burden from the past.

fillInExercises.sentence: Revising the legislation without proper consultation could open ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pandora's box' describes a source of many unpredictable and problematic consequences.

fillInExercises.sentence: By going to the press, he had ___ – there was no way to contain the story now.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Cross the Rubicon' means to pass a point of no return, committing irrevocably to a course of action.

fillInExercises.sentence: The unbeaten prosecutor finally ___ in the high-profile case against the billionaire.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Meet your Waterloo' means to face a decisive defeat or an opponent you cannot overcome.

fillInExercises.sentence: The threat of a hostile takeover hangs over the company like ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'The sword of Damocles' describes a constant, imminent threat hanging over someone.

lines.speaker: Journalist
lines.text: The Prime Minister's decision to call a snap election – has he [crossed the Rubicon|cross the Rubicon]?

lines.speaker: Analyst
lines.text: Unquestionably. There is no going back now. The party will either vindicate him or he will [meet his Waterloo|meet your Waterloo].

lines.speaker: Journalist
lines.text: And the corruption allegations – could they become [an albatross around his neck|an albatross around your neck]?

lines.speaker: Analyst
lines.text: They already are. That scandal has been [the sword of Damocles|the sword of Damocles] over the government for months.

lines.speaker: Journalist
lines.text: Has he [burned his bridges|burn your bridges] with the centrist voters by moving so far right?

lines.speaker: Analyst
lines.text: Almost certainly. And if he loses, the leadership contest could open [Pandora's box|Pandora's box] within the party.

lines.speaker: Journalist
lines.text: Quite a dramatic moment in political history.

lines.speaker: Analyst
lines.text: They always are, when leaders mistake boldness for wisdom.

lines.speaker: Consultant
lines.text: The acquisition has gone through. Have they [crossed the Rubicon|cross the Rubicon] without realising what lies ahead?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.text: I fear so. The cultural incompatibility is [an albatross around their neck|an albatross around your neck] already.

lines.speaker: Consultant
lines.text: And the regulatory exposure hangs over them like [the sword of Damocles|the sword of Damocles].

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.text: Resolving the IP dispute could open [Pandora's box|Pandora's box] of further claims from third parties.

lines.speaker: Consultant
lines.text: This may yet prove to be their [Waterloo|meet your Waterloo] in the European market.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.text: They've [burned their bridges|burn your bridges] with two major regional partners already. Recovery will not be straightforward.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: permanently destroy a relationship or path back
take an irreversible step past a point of no return
a persistent burden or source of guilt
a constant threat hanging over someone
a source of many unforeseen problems once opened

quizExercises.sentence: The unresolved lawsuit has been ___ for the company since it was filed three years ago.
quizExercises.explanation: 'The sword of Damocles' means a constant, looming threat that has not yet struck.

quizExercises.sentence: She ___ when she published the exposé. There was no returning to her former role.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Cross the Rubicon' means to take an irreversible action that commits you to a particular course.

quizExercises.question: Which scenario best illustrates 'an albatross around your neck'?
options: A new technology that transforms a company's efficiency
A decades-old financial scandal that continually resurfaces to damage a politician's reputation
An award that brings considerable prestige and recognition
quizExercises.explanation: 'An albatross around your neck' describes a persistent, inescapable burden that continuously weighs someone down.

quizExercises.question: Raising which issue would most clearly 'open Pandora's box'?
options: Resolving a small scheduling conflict between two team members
Proposing a pay equity audit that reveals years of unexplained salary discrepancies
Congratulating a colleague on a well-received presentation
quizExercises.explanation: 'Open Pandora's box' means raising something that unleashes a cascade of further, harder-to-manage problems.
